title: The Zettelkasten Method
content:
## Overview

Zettelkasten (German for "slip box") is a knowledge management method developed by sociologist Niklas Luhmann. He used it to produce over 70 books and 400 articles over his lifetime.

## Core Principles

### Atomic Notes
Each note captures one idea only — no sprawling summaries. Atomic notes are easier to link and reuse.

### Unique IDs
Every note gets a permanent, unique identifier. This decouples the note's location from its content and makes links stable.

### Links Over Hierarchy
Instead of filing notes into folders (which forces premature categorization), notes are linked to related ideas. A network of notes emerges organically.

### The Principle of Atomicity
- Write in your own words — forces genuine understanding
- One idea per note — keep it short and focused
- Link liberally — connections are the value

## Why It Works

Luhmann described his Zettelkasten as a "conversation partner" — when returning to old notes, unexpected connections surface. The system generates insights that no single note contains.

## Relevance to Brain Vault

Brain Vault's architecture is a natural digital Zettelkasten:
- File-per-note = permanent, addressable records
- `linkedNoteIds` = wikilink-style connections
- Tags provide lightweight categorization without locking notes into folders
- Real-time dashboard = instant visibility into the knowledge graph
summary: Zettelkasten is a note-taking method based on atomic, linked notes. Brain Vault's file-per-note + linkedNoteIds model implements the same principles digitally.
